narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing this path despite the challenges many face, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admissions model looks at patterns from thousands of past students to predict whether someone is likely to pass the bar exam on their first try, and unfortunately, it estimated an 82% chance that you would not—which is why your application was denied, as the school prioritizes those predicted to succeed. Your profile includes being in the lower deciles for grades in your first and third years of school (2nd and 1st percentiles compared to the typical 5th or 6th), a solid LSAT score of 37 (right around the average of 36.7), an undergraduate GPA of 2.9 (a bit below the average 3.21), high family income, and planning to attend full-time like most students. The biggest factor pushing the model toward predicting bar failure was your third-year school decile ranking, as being in the bottom of your class there strongly signaled higher risk compared to peers who ranked higher. Your first-year decile also contributed notably in the same direction, reinforcing concerns about consistent academic performance relative to other applicants. On a brighter note, your LSAT score worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success since it matches what successful students typically achieve. Your high family income surprisingly nudged the odds slightly toward failure in the model's eyes, perhaps because it weighs other academic signals more heavily regardless of background support. Your undergraduate GPA had a small push toward risk, while your full-time status offered a tiny positive influence, aligning with the vast majority of students who pass. Overall, your grades in later school years stood out as the most influential red flags when compared to typical admitted students, who tend to rank in the middle or higher deciles. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black woman leader in law—many overcome similar starts through targeted growth. To move forward, focus on boosting your academic deciles by seeking tutoring or study strategies, retake the LSAT if possible to exceed the average, and consider programs that support underrepresented students in building stronger GPAs. Reapply after addressing these, or explore schools with holistic reviews that value your unique story. You've got this—keep pushing.
